channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - kotaku.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for kotaku.com

item.title: Hit Romance Gacha Game Love And Deepspace Circumvents Strict NSFW Laws By Using Stick Figures For Horny Scenes
item.content:encoded:
Love and Deepspace, a mobile gacha game developed and published by Papergames, has run into a bit of an odd censorship problem in China during the advertising campaign for its latest in-game event, “Lingering Lust,” due to the country’s strict laws regarding NSFW content. However, after the trailer for the new event was censored twice, Papergames has resorted to a rather inventive form of artistic expression: stick figures.

OK, so, there’s a lot of conflicting information out there surrounding this, which is almost definitely even more difficult to parse due to the language barrier, but here’s what I was able to discern: Earlier this week, trailers and teasers started rolling out for the bathtub-focused Lingering Lust event, which features the female player character rolling around in the water with Love and Deepspace’s romanceable hunks.

If you’re curious what the uncensored trailer should look like, and hey, I’m not judging, here you go. It’s pretty tame, honestly, but…maybe don’t make the same mistake I did and watch it without headphones on. Also, funny aside, but the Instagram post is apparently too raunchy to embed, so enjoy a different, text-based version of the censored trailer (which I will explain later).

Love and Deepspace | Lingering Lust
Abstract Teaser — Caleb
Text Version PV#LoveandDeepspace #恋と深空 #恋与深空 #러브앤딥스페이스 pic.twitter.com/LkFIpKWoPu

— Daily Love and Deepspace (@NightingaleMiru) April 28, 2026

Now, the main trailer has seemingly been censored outright in China. From what I was able to research, Papergames actually released two censored versions of this trailer which, rather cleverly, countered the steaminess by covering parts of the visuals with steam from the bath. The first censored version wasn’t steamy enough—or, well, was still too steamy, I guess—so the second version introduced even more steam to cover up the naughtiness, to the point that the third trailer looks more akin to watching two people hotboxing inside of a Honda Civic.

But, alas, this third version was still deemed to be too raunchy. While I couldn’t find it on any of the official Chinese Love and Deepspace socials, a version of it is still available on the Love and Deepspace Taiwan Instagram.

However, the main trailer isn’t actually the one that Papergames decided to get creative with. Five separate “PV” (Player View) teaser trailers were released for the Lingering Lust event, each of which focused on one of the five individual romanceable characters in Love and Deepspace. Much like the main trailer, these were subsequently taken down, prompting Papergames to replace the men with stick figures.

Guys I’m actually crying, Love and Deepspace should do this for all trailers moving forward LMAOOOOOOOO

Yes this is an official trailer and wth did Sylus just spank MC? 🤯 https://t.co/3FN4NDC0fU

— ☆オードリーAudrey☆ (@aitaikimochi) April 29, 2026

But, if you can believe it, these were also considered too lewd for Chinese social media. I’m not kidding, by the way; the only way we know these exist is because Papergames was so proud of the stick figure trailers that they opted to upload them to every Love and Deepspace social account outside of China.

So, what is China getting instead? Text on a black screen. As you can once again see on the Love and Deepspace Taiwan Instagram page, the stick figure trailers were replaced with some sexy text on a black background, alongside the audio from said trailers, which is, frankly, way more horny than the visuals anyway. In fact, Papergames has even had to resort to using these text-based teasers on billboards in China.

As the Lingering Lust event was just released today, I haven’t been able to figure out if the actual content of the update has also been censored in China. That being said, Papergames haa had to censor in-game content in China in the past, so I’d be surprised if the event hasn’t been altered in some way.



https://kotaku.com/love-and-deepspace-game-mobile-gacha-china-sex-scenes-stick-figures-lingering-lust-2000692004
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: You’ll Miss Some Of The Best Parts Of Saros If You Don’t Die Enough
item.content:encoded:
Saros is a tough game, but if you’re just that level of Gamer that you find yourself swooping and swerving through its punishing bullet-hell firefights, you might actually be doing yourself a disservice. As crazy as it sounds, it might actually be a good idea to die on purpose a number of times, especially in the earlier sections.

Without getting into spoilers, Saros is similar to roguelike games like Hades in that a lot of its storyline happens in between runs. Each time protagonist Arjun dies, he’s sent back to the Passage, his team’s base of operations as they try to find out what happened to their settling colleagues on the hellish planet of Carcosa. When you return here, the rest of the cast will be doing their own thing, and since they’re not going out on runs with you, this is your best chance to get to know them. You’ll have conversations with them, find audio and text logs that dive into their histories, and see the Passage gradually change as time goes on. 

On paper, this is a smart structure because it gives you something to look forward to even when you fail. Saros plays with the passage of time, so sometimes you’ll return to the base and see it having changed in subtle ways, while other runs will bring you back to a pretty changed space. The trouble is, you can miss out on a lot of those details and story beats if you start off wrecking shop.

In my first playthrough, I died a collective 38 times over the course of 25 hours or so. I went back to the Passage frequently enough that I saw a lot of this stuff firsthand. In my second playthrough, I only died about 10 times, and I was struck by how quickly some of the story developments were thrown at me just because I managed to use all my game knowledge to burn through the early sections without much trouble. 

Of course, most folks will likely naturally die often enough in the first half to see what they need to see. But if you’re managing to skate through Saros with relative ease, you may be missing out on some of the most enriching character development for its larger cast. Saros makes sure you see the most important scenes to comprehend what’s going on, but death is kind of a requirement to see everything. So, I don’t know, go T-pose in front of a pack of demons a few times before finishing a zone.



https://kotaku.com/saros-story-passage-death-roguelike-arjun-kayla-sheridan-2000692091
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
